title: Wet-felted pot stand
subtitle: A thick, dense wool disc for hot pots and pans on kitchen worktops
excerpt: Wet-felt a thick round pot stand from natural-coloured merino. Fully fulled wool handles hot pots and hot dishes reliably. The finished stand is about 20 cm across and 1 cm thick when fully fulled.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional wet-felting practice. Felted-wool trivets and pot stands are a historically documented kitchen item across Scandinavian and British craft traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: A felting method using hot soapy water and agitation to lock wool fibres together without heat or water.
The vigorous agitation phase that shrinks and densifies already-felted wool. A pot stand must be fully fulled to be dense enough to hold shape under a heavy pot.
The reduction in size that occurs as wool fibres lock together and the fabric contracts. Allow 30 to 40 percent shrinkage when planning the initial roving layout for this project.

content.text: pot stand is one of the most straightforward kitchen projects for a beginner. You need only a bamboo mat, roving, hot water, and soap. The key is
the piece to full density: allow generously for
by starting with a 28 cm circle of roving to finish at about 20 cm.

Lay thin wisps of roving in a circular shape about 28 cm across on the bamboo mat. Build up four layers: layers 1 and 3 horizontal, layers 2 and 4 vertical. Keep the layers even by pulling the roving into thin sections rather than working in thick clumps.

Pour hot soapy water over the roving and press gently to saturate. Begin rubbing in circular motions with the flat of your hand, working from the centre outward. Rub for 10 minutes. The roving will knit together into a rough felt disc.

Roll the disc in the bamboo mat and roll firmly back and forth for 5 minutes. Unroll, rotate the disc 90 degrees, re-roll and roll again. Repeat the quarter-turn rolling four times. The disc will shrink and thicken visibly as you work.

For the final fulling, throw the disc onto a hard surface (the base of the sink works well) 20 to 30 times, rotating it a quarter turn between throws. This compacts the fibres more firmly than rolling alone. The finished stand should be dense, stiff, and about 1 cm thick. Rinse in cold water and leave flat to dry.

items.problem: The stand is thin and floppy rather than firm
items.cause: Under-fulling or too few roving layers
items.fix: Continue fulling by throwing and rolling until the piece is firm. If very thin, re-wet and add another two layers of roving to the surface before continuing.

items.problem: The edges of the disc have frayed or pulled in unevenly
items.cause: The edge roving was thicker than the centre or was not turned under during rubbing
items.fix: When rubbing in step 2, fold any stray edge fibres inward before rubbing over them. Re-wet if needed to keep the edge pliable during this step.
